verses.text: The ark of the LORD was in the country of the Philistines seven months. The Philistines called for the priests and the diviners, saying, What shall we do to the ark of the LORD? tell us wherewith we shall send it to his place. Guidance: Seven months of plague. The Philistines have learned by attrition what no Israelite needed to tell them: the LORD cannot be held captive. The priests and diviners — the religious experts — are now consulted, not on how to win against Israel's God but on how to surrender properly. The question 'wherewith shall we send it' is concession dressed as protocol. Even pagan worship eventually asks the right question; it just takes seven months of dying first.

The ark of the LORD was in the country of the Philistines seven months. The Philistines called for the priests and the diviners, saying, What shall we do to the ark of the LORD? tell us wherewith we shall send it to his place. Guidance: Seven months of plague. The Philistines have learned by attrition what no Israelite needed to tell them: the LORD cannot be held captive. The priests and diviners — the religious experts — are now consulted, not on how to win against Israel's God but on how to surrender properly. The question 'wherewith shall we send it' is concession dressed as protocol. Even pagan worship eventually asks the right question; it just takes seven months of dying first.

They said, If ye send away the ark of the God of Israel, send it not empty; but in any wise return him a trespass offering: then ye shall be healed, and it shall be known to you why his hand is not removed from you. Then said they, What shall be the trespass offering which we shall return to him? They answered, Five golden emerods, and five golden mice, according to the number of the lords of the Philistines: for one plague was on you all, and on your lords. Wherefore ye shall make images of your emerods, and images of your mice that mar the land; and ye shall give glory unto the God of Israel: peradventure he will lighten his hand from off you, and from off your gods, and from off your land. Wherefore then do ye harden your hearts, as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts? when he had wrought wonderfully among them, did they not let the people go, and they departed? Guidance: The Philistine priests know more Torah than expected. They prescribe a guilt offering (asham) — five golden tumors and five golden mice, one for each Philistine lord. Two diseases were running together: tumors on people, mice ravaging the land. The advice is theologically sharp: 'give glory unto the God of Israel.' Then the most striking line: 'wherefore do ye harden your hearts, as the Egyptians and Pharaoh?' Pagan priests teach pagan rulers the Pharaoh story from generations earlier — and use it as a warning. Reputation precedes the LORD. His old miracles work as long-distance evangelism whether or not the present generation has heard a sermon.

Now therefore make a new cart, and take two milch kine, on which there hath come no yoke, and tie the kine to the cart, and bring their calves home from them: and take the ark of the LORD, and lay it upon the cart; and put the jewels of gold, which ye return him for a trespass offering, in a coffer by the side thereof; and send it away, that it may go. And see, if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Beth-shemesh, then he hath done us this great evil: but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us. Guidance: The test is brilliantly designed. Take two nursing cows that have never pulled a cart, hitch them to a new (unconsecrated by other use) cart, lock up their calves at home, and let them go. Cows separated from their calves should run back to the byre. If they instead pull a cart toward Israelite territory without driver or guide, it can only be God. The Philistines build a falsifiability check; the LORD passes it. Sometimes faith is just paying attention to outcomes that no theory of chance can explain.

The men did so; and took two milch kine, and tied them to the cart, and shut up their calves at home: and they laid the ark of the LORD upon the cart, and the coffer with the mice of gold and the images of their emerods. And the kine took the straight way to the way of Beth-shemesh, and went along the highway, lowing as they went, and turned not aside to the right hand or to the left; and the lords of the Philistines went after them unto the border of Beth-shemesh. Guidance: 'Lowing as they went.' The narrator inserts the sound. The cows are bawling for their calves — every instinct screams at them to turn around — yet they head straight east. The five Philistine lords follow on foot, perhaps stunned. They have just witnessed a miracle: their own god has been refuted in the bellow of an ordinary cow. The LORD does not need a prophet's word; He uses livestock. The chapter ends Philistia's part of the story not with apology but with a procession of pagan watchmen escorting the LORD home.

They of Beth-shemesh were reaping their wheat harvest in the valley: and they lifted up their eyes, and saw the ark, and rejoiced to see it. And the cart came into the field of Joshua, a Beth-shemite, and stood there, where there was a great stone: and they clave the wood of the cart, and offered the kine a burnt offering unto the LORD. And the Levites took down the ark of the LORD, and the coffer that was with it, wherein the jewels of gold were, and put them on the great stone: and the men of Beth-shemesh offered burnt offerings and sacrificed sacrifices the same day unto the LORD. Guidance: Beth-shemesh ('house of the sun') is a priestly city in Judah's territory (Josh 21:16). The harvest workers look up and see the most precious lost object of their lifetime rolling down the road. The cart stops at a great stone in someone's field — the LORD designs His own arrival mark. The townspeople break up the cart for firewood (a single-use vehicle), kill the milch cows, and burn both as offering. The Levites — properly the only ones to handle the ark (Num 4) — lift it down. Joy is right; the action so far is right. The next verse will introduce what goes wrong.

When the five lords of the Philistines had seen it, they returned to Ekron the same day. These are the golden emerods which the Philistines returned for a trespass offering unto the LORD; for Ashdod one, for Gaza one, for Askelon one, for Gath one, for Ekron one; and the golden mice, according to the number of all the cities of the Philistines belonging to the five lords, both of fenced cities, and of country villages, even unto the great stone of Abel, whereon they set down the ark of the LORD: which stone remaineth unto this day in the field of Joshua, the Beth-shemite. Guidance: Five lords, five cities, five gold tumors. The list is precise — Ashdod, Gaza, Askelon, Gath, Ekron — the five-city Philistine confederation that menaced Israel for centuries. Their offerings now sit on a stone in a Judean wheat field. The 'great stone of Abel' (the Hebrew may simply mean 'meadow-stone') becomes a permanent monument. Every generation walking past it will hear the story: pagan kings paid to be rid of God's box. Israel's God needed no army to make Himself feared; the box alone managed Philistia's apologies.

He smote the men of Beth-shemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD, even he smote of the people fifty thousand and threescore and ten men: and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter. And the men of Beth-shemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us? Guidance: The chapter's hard turn. Beth-shemites peer inside the ark — a direct violation of Num 4:20 ('they shall not go in to see when the holy things are covered, lest they die'). The text is harsh and the number debated (some Hebrew manuscripts give the lower number 70, with 50,000 a later addition; the LXX has 70). The number probably reads '70 men, fifty chief men.' Either way, real deaths. The Philistines paid in tumors; the Israelites pay in lives because they should have known better. Familiarity with God can produce more carelessness than ignorance. The townspeople's cry — 'Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God?' — is the same fear that should have governed everyone's behavior. Holiness is not unsafe because God is mean; it is unsafe because God is holy and we are not.

They sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjath-jearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD; come ye down, and fetch it up to you. Guidance: Beth-shemesh wants the ark gone. Their own city has just learned the price of carelessness. They pass the box to Kirjath-jearim — about ten miles east, where Eleazar son of Abinadab will keep it for twenty years (7:1-2). The ark's homecoming is genuine — it is back in covenant land — but its rest is not yet complete. The chapter ends with God still in transit, neighbors passing Him along like an uncomfortable obligation. He will dwell properly only when David finally brings the ark to Jerusalem, generations later. Meanwhile the lesson is set: His presence is real, and His holiness is non-negotiable, and any community that cannot revere both should be careful what they ask Him to come home to.
